title: Dog Sudden Hind Leg Weakness and Wetting Self: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Sudden back leg weakness and loss of bladder control in dogs is a high-priority emergency. Learn the causes and why immediate vet care is required.
content_html:
What is Sudden Hind Leg Weakness and Loss of Bladder Control?

When a dog suddenly loses the ability to support weight on their back legs and simultaneously begins wetting themselves, it is usually a sign of a severe neurological event. This combination of symptoms often points to a significant disruption in the spinal cord's ability to communicate with the lower body. The loss of bladder control (urinary incontinence) alongside weakness suggests that the nerves governing both motor function and the pelvic organs are affected.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for these symptoms is High. This is considered a critical medical emergency. Conditions such as Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD), Fibrocartilaginous Embolism (FCE or 'spinal stroke'), or acute spinal trauma require immediate intervention to prevent permanent paralysis. If your dog cannot stand and is leaking urine, please contact the nearest emergency veterinary hospital or a board-certified veterinary neurologist right away. Time is of the essence to preserve nerve function.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so without delaying your trip to the vet, capturing a short video of your dog’s attempts to move can be incredibly helpful for the clinical team. A video allows the veterinarian to see the exact nature of the weakness and the dog's neurological state before the stress of the car ride or the clinic environment alters their behavior. Additionally, a photo of your dog’s resting posture can help the triage staff quickly assess the severity of the spinal involvement upon your arrival.

faqs.question: Can my dog recover from sudden hind leg paralysis?
faqs.answer: Recovery is possible and depends heavily on the underlying cause, the severity of the nerve damage, and how quickly medical or surgical treatment begins.

faqs.question: Should I try to help my dog walk?
faqs.answer: Do not force your dog to walk. Use a flat board or a thick blanket as a stretcher to keep their spine as still as possible while transporting them to the emergency vet.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Do not administer any human medications. Many over-the-counter pain relievers are toxic to dogs and can interfere with the emergency medications the veterinarian needs to use.
